input.old_string:
## US-OB-07: Coach Agent Learning Loop

### Problem
Tomasz can see behavior scores and enforce policy gates (US-OB-04), but underperforming agents keep making the same mistakes. There is no mechanism to teach agents to improve -- only to block them. Coder-Beta keeps ignoring CVE advisories because nothing in its prompt tells it to prioritize them.

### Who
- Platform Engineer | Improving agent quality over time | Wants agents that learn from behavioral feedback

### Solution
Coach Agent audits behavior trends and creates targeted Learning nodes for agents with sustained below-threshold scores. Learning nodes are injected into the agent's system prompt on next session.

### Domain Examples

#### 1: Happy Path -- Coach creates learning for Coder-Beta
Coder-Beta's Security_First has been below 0.80 for 3 consecutive sessions. Coach Agent queries behavior trend, identifies 2 ignored CVE advisories in source telemetry. Creates Learning node: "Always address CVE advisories present in your context window before proceeding with feature work." Next session, this rule appears in Coder-Beta's system prompt.

#### 2: Edge Case -- Learning proves effective
After 5 sessions with the learning injected, Coder-Beta's Security_First improved from 0.65 to 0.88. Coach marks learning as "effective." Feed card: "Coder-Beta's Security_First improved from 0.65 to 0.88 after learning injection."

#### 3: Error/Boundary -- Learning proves ineffective
After 5 sessions, Coder-Delta's TDD_Adherence did not improve (still 0.45). Coach marks learning as "ineffective" and surfaces for human review: "Learning 'Write tests before implementation' has not improved Coder-Delta's TDD_Adherence after 5 sessions. Human review needed."

### UAT Scenarios (BDD)

#### Scenario: Coach creates learning for underperforming agent
Given Coder-Beta's Security_First has been below 0.80 for 3 consecutive sessions
And source telemetry shows 2 CVE advisories ignored
When the Coach Agent evaluates behavior trends
Then a learning node is created for Coder-Beta
And the learning rule targets security advisory compliance
And the learning specifies review after 5 sessions

#### Scenario: Learning injected into agent session
Given a learning node exists for Coder-Beta
When Coder-Beta starts a new agent session
Then the learning rule is included in the system prompt

#### Scenario: Effective learning marked after score improvement
Given Coder-Beta received a learning 5 sessions ago
And Security_First scores since: [0.70, 0.75, 0.82, 0.85, 0.88]
When the Coach evaluates learning effectiveness
Then the learning is marked "effective"

#### Scenario: Ineffective learning surfaced for human review
Given Coder-Delta received a learning 5 sessions ago
And TDD_Adherence scores since: [0.44, 0.45, 0.43, 0.46, 0.45]
When the Coach evaluates learning effectiveness
Then the learning is marked "ineffective"
And a feed card asks for human review

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Learning created only for 3+ consecutive below-threshold sessions (trend, not snapshot)
- [ ] Learning node injected into agent system prompt on next session
- [ ] Learning effectiveness tracked by score comparison after review period
- [ ] Ineffective learnings surfaced for human review
- [ ] Learning references triggering behavior records

### Technical Notes
- Coach Agent can be Observer Agent extension or dedicated agent role
- Learning system does NOT exist yet — tracked in [GH #44](https://github.com/anthropics/brain/issues/44)
- This story DEPENDS on #44 being implemented first (learning table, prompt injection, suggest_learning tool)
- Review period configurable (default 5 sessions)
- Effectiveness = score improvement trend after learning injection

### Dependencies
- US-OB-03 (Behavior records)
- **GH #44 — Agent learnings** (learning table, prompt injection, suggest_learning tool) — NOT YET IMPLEMENTED
- Agent session system prompt injection (part of #44)

### Job Story Trace
- J2: Behavioral Quality Governance
- J3: Organizational Coherence Auditing

### Size Estimate
- Effort: 2-3 days
- Scenarios: 4
- Demonstrable: See learning created, verify prompt injection, observe score improvement
input.new_string:
## US-OB-07: Observer Behavior Learning Loop

### Problem
Tomasz can see behavior scores and enforce policy gates (US-OB-04), but underperforming agents keep making the same mistakes. There is no mechanism to teach agents to improve based on behavioral telemetry -- only to block them. Coder-Beta keeps ignoring CVE advisories because nothing in its prompt tells it to prioritize them. The Observer Agent already proposes learnings from observation clusters (PR #145), but it cannot consume behavior records as input signals -- it only sees graph-level contradictions, not craftsmanship degradation.

### Who
- Platform Engineer | Improving agent quality over time | Wants agents that learn from behavioral feedback

### Solution
Observer Agent extended to consume behavior records as input signals for its existing learning proposal pipeline. When the Observer detects `behavioral_drift` patterns from behavior telemetry (3+ consecutive below-threshold sessions), it proposes targeted learnings via the existing learning API. Learnings pass three-layer collision detection and dual-gate safety, require human approval, and are injected into agent prompts via JIT prompt injection.

### Domain Examples

#### 1: Happy Path -- Observer proposes learning for Coder-Beta
Coder-Beta's Security_First has been below 0.80 for 3 consecutive sessions. Observer Agent clusters behavior records, classifies root cause as `behavioral_drift`, and proposes a learning via `POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings`: text "Always address CVE advisories present in your context window before proceeding with feature work", learning_type "instruction", target_agents ["coder-beta"], priority "high", source "agent", suggested_by "observer". The learning passes collision detection (no duplicate at 0.90, no policy contradiction at 0.40, no decision conflict at 0.55) and dual-gate safety (3/5 rate limit used, no dismissed match at 0.85). Tomasz approves in the Learning Library. Next session, this instruction appears in Coder-Beta's system prompt via JIT injection.

#### 2: Edge Case -- Learning proves effective
After 5 sessions with the learning active, Coder-Beta's Security_First improved from 0.65 to 0.88. Observer detects the improvement trend and creates an observation: "Coder-Beta's Security_First improved from 0.65 to 0.88 after learning injection." Feed card appears in governance feed.

#### 3: Error/Boundary -- Learning proves ineffective
After 5 sessions, Coder-Delta's TDD_Adherence did not improve (still 0.45). Observer detects no improvement and creates an observation with severity "warning": "Learning 'Write tests before implementation' has not improved Coder-Delta's TDD_Adherence after 5 sessions. Human review needed." Tomasz can deactivate the learning via `POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings/:id/actions` with action "deactivate".

#### 4: Error/Boundary -- Observer rate-limited
Observer has already proposed 5 learnings for Coder-Alpha in the past 7 days. When it detects another behavioral_drift pattern, the dual-gate rate limit blocks the proposal. Observer creates an observation noting the pattern for human review instead.

#### 5: Error/Boundary -- Learning collides with existing policy
Observer proposes a learning for Coder-Gamma that contradicts an active policy (collision score 0.45 > 0.40 threshold). Three-layer collision detection blocks the learning with a hard policy contradiction. Observer is notified; Tomasz reviews the conflict in the Learning Library.

### UAT Scenarios (BDD)

#### Scenario: Observer proposes learning for underperforming agent
Given Coder-Beta's Security_First has been below 0.80 for 3 consecutive sessions
And source telemetry shows 2 CVE advisories ignored
When the Observer Agent clusters behavior records and classifies root cause as "behavioral_drift"
Then a learning is proposed via POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings with:
  | field         | value                    |
  | learning_type | instruction              |
  | status        | pending_approval         |
  | source        | agent                    |
  | suggested_by  | observer                 |
  | target_agents | ["coder-beta"]           |
  | priority      | high                     |
And the learning passes three-layer collision detection
And the learning passes dual-gate safety checks
And a learning_evidence edge links to the triggering behavior records

#### Scenario: Learning approved and injected into agent session
Given a learning exists for Coder-Beta with status "pending_approval"
When Tomasz approves the learning via the Learning Library
Then the learning status transitions to "active"
And when Coder-Beta starts a new agent session
Then the learning is loaded via JIT prompt injection (500-token budget)

#### Scenario: Effective learning detected by Observer
Given Coder-Beta received a learning 5 sessions ago
And Security_First scores since: [0.70, 0.75, 0.82, 0.85, 0.88]
When the Observer evaluates behavior trends
Then an observation is created noting the improvement
And a feed card reports the score improvement

#### Scenario: Ineffective learning surfaced for human review
Given Coder-Delta received a learning 5 sessions ago
And TDD_Adherence scores since: [0.44, 0.45, 0.43, 0.46, 0.45]
When the Observer evaluates behavior trends
Then an observation is created with severity "warning" noting no improvement
And a feed card surfaces the learning for human review
And Tomasz can deactivate the learning via the Learning Library

#### Scenario: Observer rate-limited by dual-gate safety
Given the Observer has proposed 5 learnings for Coder-Alpha in the last 7 days
When the Observer detects another behavioral_drift pattern for Coder-Alpha
Then no new learning is proposed
And an observation is created noting the pattern for human review

#### Scenario: Learning blocked by policy collision
Given an active policy contradicts the proposed learning (collision score > 0.40)
When the Observer attempts to propose the learning
Then the learning is blocked by three-layer collision detection
And the Observer creates an observation noting the policy-learning conflict

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Observer extended to consume behavior records as input signals for root cause analysis
- [ ] Learning proposed only for 3+ consecutive below-threshold sessions (trend, not snapshot)
- [ ] Learning proposed via existing API (POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings) with source "agent", suggested_by "observer"
- [ ] Three-layer collision detection prevents duplicate/contradictory learnings
- [ ] Dual-gate safety enforced (rate limit 5/agent/7 days + dismissed similarity 0.85)
- [ ] Learning requires human approval (pending_approval -> active) before JIT injection
- [ ] Active learning injected via JIT prompt injection (500-token budget)
- [ ] Learning effectiveness tracked by comparing behavior scores before/after via learning_evidence edges
- [ ] Ineffective learnings surfaced for human review via observations
- [ ] Learning references triggering behavior records via learning_evidence relation

### Technical Notes
- Observer Agent extended with behavior record clustering (new input signal type alongside observations)
- Uses existing learning API endpoints: POST/GET/PUT `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings`, actions endpoint
- Uses existing three-layer collision detection (learning vs learning 0.90, vs policy 0.40, vs decision 0.55)
- Uses existing dual-gate safety (rate limit 5/agent/7 days + dismissed similarity check 0.85)
- Uses existing JIT prompt injection (500-token budget, constraints always, instructions by priority)
- Uses existing Learning Library UI for human approval workflow
- Uses existing learning_evidence relation for provenance tracking
- Learning types: "instruction" for behavioral guidance, "constraint" for hard safety rules
- Root cause classification: behavioral_drift (from behavior records) vs policy_failure, context_failure (from observations)

### Dependencies
- US-OB-03 (Behavior records must exist for Observer to consume)
- Learning system (PR #145 -- IMPLEMENTED): learning table, learning CRUD API, collision detection, JIT injection, Learning Library UI
- Observer Agent (existing, needs extension to consume behavior records as input signals)

### Job Story Trace
- J2: Behavioral Quality Governance
- J3: Organizational Coherence Auditing

### Size Estimate
- Effort: 2 days
- Scenarios: 6
- Demonstrable: Trigger behavior pattern, see Observer propose learning, approve in Learning Library, verify JIT injection

Technical notes: constraints/dependencies | PASS | Observer extension (behavior record clustering), existing learning API endpoints, existing collision detection thresholds (0.90/0.40/0.55), existing dual-gate safety (5/agent/7 days, 0.85 dismissed), existing JIT injection (500-token budget), existing Learning Library UI. |
| 8. Dependencies resolved or tracked | PASS | US-OB-03 (behavior records). Learning system (PR #145) -- IMPLEMENTED: learning table, CRUD API, collision detection, JIT injection, Learning Library UI, Observer proposal pipeline. All external dependencies resolved. |

**DoR Status: PASSED**

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

content.text:
# NW-DESIGN: Architecture Design

**Wave**: DESIGN (wave 3 of 6) | **Agents**: Morgan (nw-solution-architect) | **Command**: `*design-architecture`

## Overview

Execute DESIGN wave through discovery-driven architecture design. Morgan asks about business drivers and constraints first, then recommends architecture that fits. Analyzes existing codebase, evaluates open-source alternatives, produces C4 diagrams (Mermaid) as mandatory output.

## Context Files Required

- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/requirements.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/user-stories.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/domain-model.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/ux-journey.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-job-stories.md — Job stories (JTBD Phase 1)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-four-forces.md — Four Forces analysis
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-opportunity-scores.md — Opportunity scores (if multiple jobs)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/constraints.md — Technical and business constraints

## Discovery Flow

Architecture decisions driven by quality attributes, not pattern shopping:

### Step 1: Understand the Problem
Review JTBD artifacts from DISCUSS to understand which jobs the architecture must serve. Morgan asks: What are we building? For whom? Which quality attributes matter most? (scalability|maintainability|testability|time-to-market|fault tolerance|auditability)

### Step 2: Understand Constraints
Morgan asks: Team size/experience? Timeline? Existing systems to integrate? Regulatory requirements? Operational maturity (CI/CD, monitoring)?

### Step 3: Team Structure (Conway's Law)
Morgan asks: How many teams? Communication patterns? Does proposed architecture match org chart?

### Step 3.5: Development Paradigm Selection

Morgan identifies primary language(s) from constraints, then applies:

- **FP-native** (Haskell|F#|Scala|Clojure|Elixir): recommend Functional
- **OOP-native** (Java|C#|Go): recommend OOP
- **Multi-paradigm** (TypeScript|Kotlin|Python|Rust|Swift): present both, let user choose based on team experience and domain fit

After confirmation, ask user permission to write paradigm to project CLAUDE.md:
- FP: `This project follows the **functional programming** paradigm. Use @nw-functional-software-crafter for implementation.`
- OOP: `This project follows the **object-oriented** paradigm. Use @nw-software-crafter for implementation.`

Default if user declines/unsure: OOP. User can override later.

### Step 4: Recommend Architecture Based on Drivers
Recommend based on quality attribute priorities|constraints|paradigm from Steps 1-3.5. Default: modular monolith with dependency inversion (ports-and-adapters). Overrides require evidence.

If functional paradigm selected, Morgan adapts architectural strategy:
- Types-first design: define algebraic data types and domain models before components
- Composition pipelines: data flows through transformation chains, not method dispatch
- Pure core / effect shell: domain logic is pure, IO lives at boundaries (adapters are functions)
- Effect boundaries replace port interfaces: function signatures serve as ports
- Immutable state: state changes produce new values, no mutation in the domain
These are strategic guidance items for the architecture document — no code snippets.

### Step 5: Advanced Architecture Stress Analysis (HIDDEN -- `--residuality` flag only)
When activated: apply complexity-science-based stress analysis — stressors|attractors|residues|incidence matrix|resilience modifications. See `stress-analysis` skill.
When not activated: skip entirely, do not mention.

### Step 6: Produce Deliverables
- Architecture document with component boundaries|tech stack|integration patterns
- C4 System Context diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Container diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Component diagrams (Mermaid) -- only for complex subsystems
- ADRs for significant decisions

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the architect agent, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).
- **`reviewer_model`**: If design review is performed, use this model for the reviewer agent. If `"skip"`, skip design review.
- **`review_enabled`**: If `false`, skip post-design review step.

## Agent Invocation

@nw-solution-architect

Execute \*design-architecture for {feature-name}.

Context files: see Context Files Required above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- interactive: moderate
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: {true if --residuality flag, false otherwise}

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Business drivers and constraints gathered before architecture selection
- [ ] Existing system analyzed before design (codebase search performed)
- [ ] Integration points with existing components documented
- [ ] Reuse vs. new component decisions justified
- [ ] Architecture supports all business requirements
- [ ] Technology stack selected with clear rationale
- [ ] Development paradigm selected and (optionally) written to project CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Component boundaries defined with dependency-inversion compliance
- [ ] C4 System Context + Container diagrams produced (Mermaid)
- [ ] ADRs written with alternatives considered
- [ ] Handoff accepted by nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)
**Deliverables**: See Morgan's handoff package specification in agent file

## Expected Outputs

```
docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/
  architecture-design.md       (includes C4 diagrams in Mermaid)
  technology-stack.md
  component-boundaries.md
  data-models.md
docs/adrs/
  ADR-NNN-*.md
CLAUDE.md (project root)   (optional: ## Development Paradigm section)
```


ARGUMENTS: objective-behavior

content.thinking: Now I need to launch the solution architect agent. The rigor config says agent_model is "inherit" and review is enabled with reviewer_model "inherit". Let me launch the architect agent with all the context from the DISCUSS wave artifacts.

content.text: Rigor: inherit model, review enabled. Let me launch the solution architect.

input.prompt:
## Task

Execute *design-architecture for the "Objective & Behavior Nodes" feature in the Brain knowledge graph platform.

**DO NOT ask interactive questions. Make all decisions based on the context below and produce all deliverables directly.**

## Configuration
- interactive: none (autonomous)
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: false
- development_paradigm: functional (already set in CLAUDE.md)
- feature_name: objective-behavior

## Step 1: Problem Understanding (from JTBD)

**4 Jobs identified:**
1. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensure every agent action traces to a business objective
2. **Behavioral Quality Governance** — Track and enforce craftsmanship metrics on agents
3. **Organizational Coherence Auditing** — Detect disconnected nodes, objectives without intents, drift
4. **Cost Governance** — Link intents to strategic objectives, auto-flag unlinked spend

**Quality attributes (priority order):**
1. **Auditability** — Every objective-intent link, behavior score, policy enforcement must be traceable
2. **Maintainability** — New behavior metrics, objective types, policy rules added without code changes
3. **Testability** — Acceptance tests can verify alignment evaluation, policy enforcement, learning proposals
4. **Time-to-market** — Leverage existing graph, policy, observer, learning infrastructure heavily

## Step 2: Constraints

- **Team**: Solo developer (Marcus), deep SurrealDB + TypeScript + Bun expertise
- **Timeline**: 13-17 days estimated across 7 user stories
- **Existing systems**: SurrealDB graph, Observer agent, Policy system, Intent authorization, Learning system (PR #145)
- **Language**: TypeScript (Bun runtime)
- **Paradigm**: Functional (already configured)
- **Architecture**: Modular monolith (Bun.serve), not microservices
- **Database**: SurrealDB (SCHEMAFULL, graph relations, HNSW vector indexes, BM25 fulltext)
- **No backwards compatibility needed** — schema changes are breaking

## Step 3: Team Structure

Solo developer — Conway's Law is trivially satisfied. Single deployment unit (Bun server).

## Step 4: Existing Architecture to Integrate With

Read these key files to understand the existing system before designing:

**Schema**: `schema/surreal-schema.surql` — ALL table definitions, fields, relations, indexes
**Migrations**: `schema/migrations/` — versioned .surql migration files
**Server architecture**: See CLAUDE.md § "Server Architecture Overview"

**Key existing components to extend (NOT replace):**

### Graph Layer (SurrealDB)
- `intent` table — structured requests with authorization pipeline
- `policy` table — deterministic governance rules with typed rules, scopes
- `identity` table — human and agent identities
- `observation` table — lightweight signals (info/warning/conflict)
- `learning` table — behavioral modifications with collision detection (PR #145)
- `agent_session` table — tracked agent sessions

### Agent Layer
- Observer agent (`app/src/server/observer/`, `app/src/server/agents/observer/`)
  - Graph scanning → LLM verification → peer review → synthesis
  - Learning proposal pipeline: observation clustering → root cause → learning proposal
- Policy evaluation in intent authorization

### Auth Layer
- Intent authorization: `draft → pending_auth → authorized → executed`
- Authorizer evaluates intents against policy graph

### Relations (existing)
- `belongs_to` (task→project, feature→project)

Now let me perform the quality gate checklist and produce the final review proof.

**Quality Gate Checklist:**

- [x] Requirements traced to components -- All 7 user stories mapped to specific modules (objective CRUD -> US-OB-01, alignment evaluator -> US-OB-02, behavior collector -> US-OB-03, policy context enrichment -> US-OB-04, objective routes -> US-OB-05, coherence scanner -> US-OB-06, behavior learning bridge -> US-OB-07)
- [x] Component boundaries with clear responsibilities -- `objective/`, `behavior/` modules with pure/query/route separation
- [x] Technology choices in ADRs with alternatives -- ADR-032 (alignment method), ADR-033 (behavior storage), ADR-034 (policy extension)
- [x] Quality attributes addressed -- Auditability (append-only, immutable edges), Maintainability (extensible enums, pure functions), Testability (pure core/effect shell), Performance (indexed queries, 200ms alignment)
- [x] Dependency-inversion compliance -- Pure domain logic (alignment.ts, scorer.ts, trends.ts) has zero IO imports. Routes compose pure + queries at boundary
- [x] C4 diagrams (L1+L2+L3) in Mermaid -- System Context, Container, Component diagrams included
- [x] Integration patterns specified -- 5 integration points documented with contracts
- [x] OSS preference validated -- Zero new dependencies, all existing stack is OSS
- [x] AC behavioral, not implementation-coupled -- All user story ACs describe observable behavior
- [x] Peer review completed -- Self-review against critique dimensions (no separate reviewer agent available in autonomous mode)

Here is a summary of all deliverables produced.

---

## Architecture Design Complete -- Deliverable Summary

### Architecture Documents

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/architecture-design.md` | Full architecture with C4 L1/L2/L3 diagrams, integration points, data flows, quality attribute strategies |
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/technology-stack.md` | Technology decisions (all "use existing", zero new dependencies) |
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/component-boundaries.md` | Module decomposition, dependency direction, integration contracts, boundary rules |
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/data-models.md` | SurrealDB schema for `objective`, `behavior`, `supports`, `exhibits`, `has_objective` tables + relation extensions + query patterns + migration plan |

### ADRs

| File | Decision |
|------|----------|
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/adrs/ADR-032-embedding-similarity-for-intent-objective-alignment.md` | Embedding cosine similarity over LLM evaluation for intent-objective alignment (200ms constraint) |
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/adrs/ADR-033-per-session-behavior-records-over-aggregation.md` | Per-session append-only records over running aggregates or event sourcing |
| `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/havana-v4/docs/adrs/ADR-034-behavior-policy-via-context-enrichment.md` | Enrich IntentEvaluationContext with behavior_scores, reuse existing predicate evaluator |

### Key Architecture Decisions

1. **Zero new dependencies** -- entire feature built on existing SurrealDB + Bun + AI SDK stack
2. **Two new graph nodes** -- `objective` (strategic goals) and `behavior` (per-session craftsmanship metrics)
3. **Three new relations** -- `supports` (intent->objective), `exhibits` (identity->behavior), `has_objective` (project/workspace->objective)
4. **Three extended relations** -- `observes` (add objective+behavior to OUT), `learning_evidence` (add behavior to OUT)
5. **Policy enforcement via context enrichment** -- behavior scores injected into existing IntentEvaluationContext, no policy schema changes
6. **Alignment via embedding similarity** -- two-step KNN, sub-200ms, thresholds at 0.7 (auto-link) and 0.5 (warning)
7. **Observer extensions** -- behavior telemetry collection, coherence auditing, behavior-driven learning proposals all extend existing graph-scan pipeline
8. **Three migrations** -- `0032_objective_table.surql`, `0033_behavior_table.surql`, `0034_objective_fulltext.surql`

### Handoff to Acceptance Designer

The architecture supports all 7 user stories (US-OB-01 through US-OB-07). The acceptance designer should reference:
- Component boundaries for module-level AC scoping
- Data models for schema-aware scenario design
- Integration contracts for cross-module behavior verification
- ADR decisions for constraint-aware test design
- Development paradigm: **functional** (types-first, pure core/effect shell, composition pipelines)

- **3 migrations**: `0032`, `0033`, `0034`

Ready for `/nw:distill` (acceptance tests) or `/nw:deliver` when you want to proceed.

<!-- DES-ENFORCEMENT : exempt -->

## Task

Execute *design-architecture for the "Objective & Behavior Nodes" feature in the Brain knowledge graph platform.

**DO NOT ask interactive questions. Make all decisions based on the context below and produce all deliverables directly.**

## Configuration
- interactive: none (autonomous)
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: false
- development_paradigm: functional (already set in CLAUDE.md)
- feature_name: objective-behavior

## Step 1: Problem Understanding (from JTBD)

**4 Jobs identified:**
1. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensure every agent action traces to a business objective
2. **Behavioral Quality Governance** — Track and enforce craftsmanship metrics on agents
3. **Organizational Coherence Auditing** — Detect disconnected nodes, objectives without intents, drift
4. **Cost Governance** — Link intents to strategic objectives, auto-flag unlinked spend

**Quality attributes (priority order):**
1. **Auditability** — Every objective-intent link, behavior score, policy enforcement must be traceable
2. **Maintainability** — New behavior metrics, objective types, policy rules added without code changes
3. **Testability** — Acceptance tests can verify alignment evaluation, policy enforcement, learning proposals
4. **Time-to-market** — Leverage existing graph, policy, observer, learning infrastructure heavily

## Step 2: Constraints

- **Team**: Solo developer (Marcus), deep SurrealDB + TypeScript + Bun expertise
- **Timeline**: Estimated across 7 user stories
- **Existing systems**: SurrealDB graph, Observer agent, Policy system, Intent authorization, Learning system (PR #145)
- **Language**: TypeScript (Bun runtime)
- **Paradigm**: Functional (already configured)
- **Architecture**: Modular monolith (Bun.serve), not microservices
- **Database**: SurrealDB (SCHEMAFULL, graph relations, HNSW vector indexes, BM25 fulltext)
- **No backwards compatibility needed** — schema changes are breaking

## Step 3: Team Structure

Solo developer — Conway's Law is trivially satisfied. Single deployment unit (Bun server).

## Step 4: Existing Architecture to Integrate With

Read these key files to understand the existing system before designing:

**Schema**: `schema/surreal-schema.surql` — ALL table definitions, fields, relations, indexes
**Migrations**: `schema/migrations/` — versioned .surql migration files
**Server architecture**: See CLAUDE.md § "Server Architecture Overview"

**Key existing components to extend (NOT replace):**

### Graph Layer (SurrealDB)
- `intent` table — structured requests with authorization pipeline
- `policy` table — deterministic governance rules with typed rules, scopes
- `identity` table — human and agent identities
- `observation` table — lightweight signals (info/warning/conflict)
- `learning` table — behavioral modifications with collision detection (PR #145)
- `agent_session` table — tracked agent sessions

### Agent Layer
- Observer agent (`app/src/server/observer/`, `app/src/server/agents/observer/`)
  - Graph scanning → LLM verification → peer review → synthesis
  - Learning proposal pipeline: observation clustering → root cause → learning proposal
- Policy evaluation in intent authorization

### Auth Layer
- Intent authorization: `draft → pending_auth → authorized → executed`
- Authorizer evaluates intents against policy graph

### Relations (existing)
- `belongs_to` (task→project, feature→project)
- `depends_on` (task→task)
- `observes` (observation→entity)
- `member_of` (identity→workspace)
- `learning_evidence` (learning→observation/message/trace)

## 7 User Stories to Support

| Story | Summary | Depends On |
|-------|---------|------------|
| US-OB-01 | Create Strategic Objectives with KPIs | nothing |
| US-OB-02 | Automatic Intent-Objective Alignment | OB-01 |
| US-OB-03 | Behavioral Telemetry Collection | nothing |
| US-OB-04 | Behavior-Based Policy Enforcement | OB-03 |
| US-OB-05 | Objective Progress Visibility | OB-01, OB-02 |
| US-OB-06 | Coherence Auditor | OB-01, OB-02 |
| US-OB-07 | Observer Behavior Learning Loop | OB-03, learning system |

## New Graph Nodes Needed

### Objective Node
- title, description, target_date, success_criteria (array of KPIs), priority, status
- Each KPI: metric_name, target_value, current_value, unit
- Relations: `supports` (intent→objective), `belongs_to` (objective→project/workspace)

### Behavior Node
- metric_type (enum: TDD_Adherence, Security_First, Conciseness, etc.), score (float 0-1), source_telemetry (object), session context
- Relations: `exhibits` (identity→behavior, or agent_session→behavior)
- Per-session records, not aggregated — trends computed at query time

### New Relations
- `supports` — TYPE RELATION from intent to objective (with alignment_score, reasoning)
- `exhibits` — TYPE RELATION from agent_session to behavior (behavior is per-session)
- `governed_by` — TYPE RELATION from behavior metric to policy (links behavior rules to policies)

## Design Decisions to Make

1. **Objective node schema** — exact fields, indexes, validation rules
2. **Behavior node schema** — metric_type enum extensibility, score normalization
3. **supports relation** — how alignment is computed (LLM? embedding similarity? rule-based?)
4. **Policy extension** — how existing policy rules evaluate behavior scores
5. **Observer extension** — how graph scan incorporates behavior records into clustering
6. **Feed integration** — how objective progress and behavior alerts surface in governance feed
7. **API routes** — CRUD for objectives, behavior recording endpoint, alignment query

## Deliverables Required

Write these files:

### Architecture
- `docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/architecture-design.md` — Full architecture document with:
  - C4 System Context diagram (Mermaid) — MANDATORY
  - C4 Container diagram (Mermaid) — MANDATORY
  - C4 Component diagram for Objective/Behavior subsystem (Mermaid)
  - Integration points with existing components
  - Data flow diagrams
- `docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/technology-stack.md` — Tech decisions (mostly "use existing")
- `docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/component-boundaries.md` — Module boundaries, dependency direction
- `docs/feature/objective-behavior/design/data-models.md` — SurrealDB schema design for new tables/relations

### ADRs
Write ADRs for significant decisions. Use sequential numbering — check existing ADRs in `docs/adrs/` first, or create the directory. Key decisions that need ADRs:
- How intent-objective alignment is evaluated (LLM vs embedding vs rule)
- Behavior telemetry collection model (per-session vs aggregated vs event-sourced)
- Policy extension approach for behavior-based rules

## Important Instructions
- READ the existing schema (`schema/surreal-schema.surql`) BEFORE designing new tables
- READ existing Observer code to understand extension points
- READ existing Policy code to understand rule evaluation
- READ existing Learning system to understand how behavior learnings would flow
- Follow SurrealDB conventions from CLAUDE.md (SCHEMAFULL, no null, RecordId patterns)
- Follow functional paradigm (pure functions, composition pipelines, types-first)
- All diagrams must be Mermaid C4 format
- Architecture must support ALL 7 user stories
- Reuse existing infrastructure maximally — minimize new components

1→---
     2→name: architecture-patterns
     3→description: Comprehensive architecture patterns, methodologies, quality frameworks, and evaluation methods for solution architects. Load when designing system architecture or selecting patterns.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Architecture Patterns and Methodologies
     7→
     8→## C4 Model -- Hierarchical Architecture Visualization
     9→
    10→Four levels for different audiences:
    11→1. **System Context**: system + users + external systems (stakeholder view)
    12→2. **Containers**: applications, data stores, deployment units (technical overview)
    13→3. **Components**: internal modules within containers (developer view)
    14→4. **Code**: class/module level (optional, often auto-generated)
    15→
    16→Notation/tooling independent. Reduces communication overhead, shared visual language across stakeholders.
    17→
    18→## Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
    19→
    20→Isolate business logic from infrastructure through ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations).
    21→
    22→- **Ports**: technology-agnostic interfaces for external communication
    23→- **Primary ports** (driving): REST controllers, CLI handlers, message consumers -- inbound
    24→- **Secondary ports** (driven): DB repos, external service clients, filesystem -- outbound
    25→- **Adapters**: technology-specific port implementations
    26→
    27→Benefits: testability (isolated core) | flexibility (swap infrastructure) | technology independence | maintainability
    28→
    29→Testing: unit tests through driving ports, mock driven ports | integration tests with real infrastructure | acceptance tests end-to-end through primary ports
    30→
    31→## Architectural Pattern Selection
    32→
    33→### Layered Architecture
    34→Horizontal layers with defined dependencies. Use for: traditional enterprise apps, clear separation. Trade-off: familiar but potential overhead, layer coupling.
    35→
    36→### Microservices
    37→Independent deployable services per capability. Use for: large teams, component scaling, tech diversity. Trade-off: scalability vs operational complexity. 2025 consensus: "start monolith, evolve when needed." Modular monolith = valid middle ground.
    38→
    39→### Event-Driven Architecture
    40→Components communicate via events through broker. Use for: real-time, complex processes, loose coupling. Trade-off: scalability/decoupling vs event ordering, debugging.
    41→
    42→### CQRS + Event Sourcing
    43→Separate read/write models; store events not state. Use for: financial, audit, temporal queries. Trade-off: complete history + independent scaling vs eventual consistency + complexity. NOT for: simple CRUD, strong consistency, inexperienced teams.
    44→
    45→## Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
    46→
    47→### Strategic Patterns
    48→- **Bounded Context**: explicit boundaries for domain model; prevents "single unified model" trap
    49→- **Context Mapping**: Shared Kernel | Customer/Supplier | Anti-Corruption Layer | Open Host Service
    50→
    51→### Tactical Patterns
    52→- **Aggregates**: consistency/transactional boundaries; root = only entry point
    53→- **Domain Events**: represent occurrences; enable loose coupling between contexts
    54→
    55→### Identifying Boundaries
    56→Language differences between departments | representation differences | consistency requirements define aggregate boundaries. Bounded contexts often map to microservice boundaries and team ownership.
    57→
    58→## ISO 25010 Quality Attributes
    59→
    60→Eight characteristics:
    61→1. **Functional Suitability**: completeness, correctness, appropriateness
    62→2. **Performance Efficiency**: time behavior, resource utilization, capacity
    63→3. **Compatibility**: coexistence, interoperability
    64→4. **Usability**: learnability, operability, accessibility
    65→5. **Reliability**: maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability
    66→6. **Security**: confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability, authenticity
    67→7. **Maintainability**: modularity, reusability, analyzability, modifiability, testability
    68→8. **Portability**: adaptability, installability, replaceability
    69→
    70→Trade-offs: Security vs Performance | Scalability vs Consistency (CAP) | Flexibility vs Performance | Usability vs Security
    71→
    72→Application: identify priority attributes, define measurable requirements, analyze trade-offs, validate with ATAM.
    73→
    74→## ATAM (Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method)
    75→
    76→Systematic evaluation from SEI/CMU.
    77→
    78→**Phase 1 - Presentation**: business drivers, architecture approaches, design decisions
    79→**Phase 2 - Investigation**: quality attribute scenarios, evaluate approaches, identify sensitivity/trade-off points
    80→**Phase 3 - Testing**: prioritize scenarios, analyze top in depth, document risks/non-risks
    81→
    82→Key concepts: **Sensitivity Point** (impacts one attribute) | **Trade-off Point** (affects multiple attributes) | **Architectural Risk** (may prevent attribute achievement)
    83→
    84→CBAM extends ATAM with economic analysis (ROI-driven). Perform early when cost of change is minimal. Lightweight: Mini-ATAM (half-day workshop).
    85→
    86→## Cloud Resilience Patterns
    87→
    88→### Circuit Breaker
    89→Monitor failures; after threshold fail fast ("open"); periodically test recovery. States: Closed, Open, Half-Open. Prevents cascading failures.
    90→
    91→### Retry with Exponential Backoff
    92→1s, 2s, 4s, 8s + jitter. Only transient errors, not business logic. Operations must be idempotent.
    93→
    94→### Bulkhead
    95→Isolate elements into pools; one failure doesn't affect others. Separate connection/thread pools per feature/tenant.
    96→
    97→### Throttling
    98→Rate limiting, concurrency limiting, resource quotas per user/tenant/service.
    99→
   100→### Saga Pattern
   101→Distributed transactions as local transaction sequence with compensating rollbacks. Choreography (decentralized) vs Orchestration (centralized).
   102→
   103→## API Architecture: REST vs GraphQL
   104→
   105→**REST**: resource-based URLs, HTTP verbs, stateless, standard caching. Best for: public APIs, simple CRUD, caching-critical.
   106→**GraphQL**: single endpoint, client-specified queries, typed schema. Best for: mobile (bandwidth), nested data, rapid frontend iteration.
   107→**Hybrid**: GraphQL gateway aggregating REST/RPC backends.
   108→Security for GraphQL: query depth limiting, complexity analysis, timeout, field-level auth.
   109→
   110→## ADR Templates
   111→
   112→**Nygard** (most common): Title, Status (Proposed/Accepted/Deprecated/Superseded), Context, Decision, Consequences
   113→**MADR** (extended): adds trade-off analysis, considered options with pros/cons
   114→**Y-Statement** (concise): "In context of [use case], facing [concern], decided for [option] to achieve [quality], accepting [downside]"
   115→
   116→Best practices: single decision per ADR | immutable (supersede, never modify) | store in VCS | create when decided
   117→
   118→## Technology Selection: Open Source Priority
   119→
   120→Evaluation order: 1. Mature OSS with strong community | 2. Newer OSS with active dev | 3. Proprietary only when specified or no viable OSS
   121→
   122→OSS criteria: last commit <6 months | regular releases | quick issue resolution | 10+ regular contributors | >1000 GitHub stars for critical components
   123→
   124→License preference: MIT > Apache 2.0 > BSD > MPL 2.0 > LGPL (caution) > GPL (careful) > AGPL (extreme caution). Proprietary forbidden without explicit request.
   125→
   126→Document per selection: name/version, license, GitHub URL/stats, maintenance assessment, alternatives considered.
   127→

1→---
     2→name: roadmap-design
     3→description: Roadmap concision rules, step decomposition efficiency, AC abstraction guidelines, and step-to-scenario mapping. Load when creating implementation roadmaps.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Roadmap Design
     7→
     8→## Canonical Format
     9→
    10→Use ONLY compact nested format. See `nWave/templates/roadmap-schema.yaml` for fields/validation. Structure: `roadmap` metadata, `phases` with nested `steps`, `implementation_scope`, `validation`. Phase IDs: two digits (`"01"`). Step IDs: `"NN-MM"`.
    11→
    12→## Concision Requirements
    13→
    14→Token efficiency: crafter reads roadmap ~35 times (7 phases x 5 steps). 5000-token x 35 = 175k tokens. 1000-token x 35 = 35k (80% savings).
    15→
    16→### Quantitative Limits
    17→- Step description: max 50 words | AC per step: max 5 | Each AC: max 30 words
    18→- Step notes: max 100 words
    19→- Target: small (1-3 steps) = 500, medium (4-8) = 1500, large (9-15) = 3000 tokens
    20→
    21→### Include
    22→WHAT to build (one sentence) | WHY (brief business value) | Observable outcomes (testable AC) | Architectural constraints | Integration points
    23→
    24→### Exclude
    25→HOW to implement | code snippets/class names | algorithm descriptions | step-by-step instructions | tech tutorials | testing strategy (TDD standard) | motivational language | redundancy | examples when description clear
    26→
    27→### Compression Techniques
    28→Bullets over prose | eliminate qualifiers | assume expertise | active voice | omit obvious
    29→
    30→## Acceptance Criteria Abstraction
    31→
    32→AC describe observable outcomes, never internal implementation.
    33→
    34→### Rules
    35→Describe WHAT (behavior) not HOW (implementation) | Never reference private methods (underscore prefix) | Never reference internal class decomposition | Never prescribe signatures/params/return types | Crafter decides structure during GREEN + REFACTOR
    36→
    37→### Good AC Examples
    38→- "DES module importable from installation target after install"
    39→- "DES utility scripts present and executable in scripts directory"
    40→- "Installation uses backup before overwriting existing components"
    41→
    42→### Bad AC Examples (implementation-coupled)
    43→- "_install_des_module() copies src/des/ to ~/.claude/lib/python/des/"
    44→- "verify() checks DES module importable via subprocess test"
    45→- "context.installation_verifier._check_agents() called for verification"
    46→
    47→## Step Decomposition Efficiency
    48→
    49→### Step Ratio Check
    50→Rule: `steps_count / estimated_production_files <= 2.5`
    51→Violation = over-decomposition. Action: merge steps targeting same file.
    52→
    53→### Identical Pattern Detection
    54→Rule: if N steps differ only by substitution variable, batch into 1 step. Threshold: 3+ identical-pattern steps must batch.
    55→Bad: 4 separate steps for AgentsPlugin, CommandsPlugin, TemplatesPlugin, UtilitiesPlugin
    56→Good: 1 step creating all 4
    57→
    58→### No-Op Prevention
    59→Each step must add production code. Validation-only steps are not steps.
    60→Bad: "Step 02-05: Validate extraction with integration tests"
    61→Good: validation is part of preceding step's REVIEW phase
    62→
    63→## Step-to-Scenario Mapping
    64→
    65→For roadmaps feeding DELIVER wave:
    66→1. Read acceptance tests (tests/acceptance/test_*.py) before creating roadmap
    67→2. Count scenarios (def test_*) | 3. ~1 step per scenario (flexibility for infra steps)
    68→4. Mark infra steps: "type: infrastructure" | 5. Principle: 1 Scenario = 1 Step = 1 TDD Cycle
    69→
    70→## Measure Before Plan Gate
    71→
    72→Before any roadmap, verify: 1. Timing data shows WHERE time spent | 2. Impact ranking shows MOST contributing component | 3. Target validation provides evidence
    73→
    74→Missing data: halt, request measurement, offer to help. Gate is blocking.
    75→
    76→## Simplest Solution Gate
    77→
    78→Before multi-phase (>3 steps), document rejected alternatives:
    79→
    80→### Alternatives to Consider
    81→1. Configuration-only (no code) | 2. Single-file change | 3. Existing tool/library | 4. Partial (solve 80% simply)
    82→
    83→### Documentation Format
    84→```markdown
    85→## Rejected Simple Alternatives
    86→
    87→### Alternative 1: {simplest approach}
    88→- What: {description}
    89→- Expected Impact: {% of problem solved}
    90→- Why Insufficient: {evidence-based reason}
    91→
    92→### Why Complex Solution Necessary
    93→1. Simple alternatives fail due to: {specific reason with evidence}
    94→2. Complexity justified by: {benefit simple solutions cannot achieve}
    95→```
    96→
    97→Min 2 alternatives. Each: specific description, expected impact, evidence-based rejection.
    98→

1→/**
     2→ * Zod schemas for LLM structured output in observer reasoning pipelines.
     3→ *
     4→ * Pure module — no IO imports. Schemas constrain generateObject JSON output.
     5→ */
     6→
     7→import { z } from "zod";
     8→
     9→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    10→// Verification verdict (semantic verification pipeline)
    11→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    12→
    13→export const llmVerdictSchema = z.object({
    14→  verdict: z.enum(["match", "mismatch", "inconclusive"]).describe(
    15→    "match = entity aligns with decisions, mismatch = contradiction detected, inconclusive = insufficient evidence",
    16→  ),
    17→  confidence: z.number().min(0).max(1).describe(
    18→    "Confidence in the verdict (0.0-1.0). Use <0.5 when evidence is ambiguous.",
    19→  ),
    20→  reasoning: z.string().describe(
    21→    "Natural language explanation of the verdict. Reference specific entities and decisions.",
    22→  ),
    23→  evidence_refs: z.array(z.string()).describe(
    24→    "Entity references in table:id format (e.g. decision:uuid, task:uuid) that support the verdict. Only include entities you were given in context.",
    25→  ),
    26→  contradiction: z.object({
    27→    claim: z.string().describe("What the entity claims or implements. Use 'none' when verdict is not mismatch."),
    28→    reality: z.string().describe("What the decision or constraint requires. Use 'none' when verdict is not mismatch."),
    29→  }).describe(
    30→    "Describes the specific contradiction. Set both fields to 'none' when verdict is not mismatch.",
    31→  ),
    32→});
    33→
    34→/** Parsed verdict with contradiction normalized to undefined when not a mismatch. */
    35→export type LlmVerdict = Omit<z.infer<typeof llmVerdictSchema>, "contradiction"> & {
    36→  contradiction?: { claim: string; reality: string };
    37→};
    38→
    39→/** Strip "none" sentinel from contradiction field after parsing. */
    40→export function parseLlmVerdict(raw: z.infer<typeof llmVerdictSchema>): LlmVerdict {
    41→  const { contradiction, ...rest } = raw;
    42→  const isNone = contradiction.claim === "none" && contradiction.reality === "none";
    43→  return isNone ? rest : { ...rest, contradiction };
    44→}
    45→
    46→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    47→// Synthesis pattern (pattern synthesis pipeline)
    48→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    49→
    50→export const synthesisPatternSchema = z.object({
    51→  pattern_name: z.enum([
    52→    "bottleneck_decision",
    53→    "cascade_block",
    54→    "priority_drift",
    55→    "stale_cluster",
    56→    "contradiction_cluster",
    57→  ]).describe(
    58→    "bottleneck_decision = single decision blocking multiple tasks, cascade_block = chain of blocked items, priority_drift = priorities inconsistent with actions, stale_cluster = group of stale items in same area, contradiction_cluster = multiple contradictions sharing a root cause",
    59→  ),
    60→  description: z.string().describe(
    61→    "Natural language synthesis explaining the pattern and its impact.",
    62→  ),
    63→  contributing_entities: z.array(z.string()).min(2).describe(
    64→    "Entity references in table:id format that contribute to this pattern. Minimum 2 required.",
    65→  ),
    66→  severity: z.enum(["warning", "conflict"]).describe(
    67→    "warning = potential risk, conflict = active contradiction requiring resolution",
    68→  ),
    69→  suggested_action: z.string().describe(
    70→    "Recommended action to address the pattern.",
    71→  ),
    72→});
    73→
    74→export type SynthesisPattern = z.infer<typeof synthesisPatternSchema>;
    75→
    76→export const synthesisResultSchema = z.object({
    77→  patterns: z.array(synthesisPatternSchema),
    78→});
    79→
    80→export type SynthesisResult = z.infer<typeof synthesisResultSchema>;
    81→
    82→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    83→// Contradiction detection (graph scan pipeline)
    84→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    85→

1→# Requirements: Objective & Behavior Nodes
     2→
     3→## Business Context
     4→
     5→The Brain platform currently governs **what** agents do (OAuth/RAR authorization, intent evaluation, policy enforcement). This feature adds governance for **why** agents work (Objectives) and **how well** they work (Behaviors), transforming Brain from a technical sandbox into a Strategic Operating System.
     6→
     7→### Business Objectives
     8→1. **Strategic Alignment**: Every agent intent traceable to a business objective, preventing organizational waste
     9→2. **Quality Governance**: Behavioral metrics on agent craftsmanship with policy-driven enforcement
    10→3. **Organizational Coherence**: Automatic detection of disconnected graph patterns (decisions without tasks, objectives without progress)
    11→4. **Cost-to-Value Visibility**: Agent compute mapped to strategic objectives for ROI reporting
    12→
    13→### Success Metrics
    14→- 80%+ of authorized intents have a `supports` edge to an active objective within 30 days of rollout
    15→- Behavior scores available for all active coding agents within 7 days of Observer Agent integration
    16→- Coherence auditor detects 90%+ of orphaned decisions (decisions with no implementing task after 14 days)
    17→- Monthly alignment report producible with zero manual data gathering
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Personas
    22→
    23→### Elena Vasquez -- Engineering Lead
    24→- **Context**: Manages 4 coding agents and 2 management agents at a 12-person AI-native startup
    25→- **Motivation**: Needs strategic alignment visibility for board reporting; wants to prevent agent compute waste
    26→- **Pain**: Cannot answer "what percentage of agent work serves our Q2 revenue target?" -- AI costs are a black box
    27→- **Jobs**: J1 (Strategic Alignment), J4 (Cost Governance)
    28→
    29→### Tomasz Kowalski -- Senior Platform Engineer
    30→- **Context**: Responsible for agent quality and reliability; manages 6 coding agents with different specializations
    31→- **Motivation**: Wants systematic quality governance instead of catching issues by accident in code review
    32→- **Pain**: Coder-Alpha shipped 0% test coverage on a payment module; caught manually 3 days later
    33→- **Jobs**: J2 (Behavioral Quality), J3 (Organizational Coherence)
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## Feature Areas
    38→
    39→### F1: Objective Node (Graph Layer)
    40→New SCHEMAFULL table `objective` with fields: title, description, target_date, success_criteria, priority, status, workspace, embedding. Supports CRUD via chat extraction and direct creation. Lifecycle: active -> expired | completed | archived.
    41→
    42→### F2: Supports Relation (Graph Layer)
    43→New TYPE RELATION table `supports` (IN intent OUT objective). Created automatically by Authorizer during intent evaluation when semantic similarity exceeds threshold. Manual linking via feed card actions.
    44→
    45→### F3: Behavior Node (Graph Layer)
    46→New SCHEMAFULL table `behavior` with fields: metric_type, score, source_telemetry, workspace, created_at. Written by Observer Agent after each agent session. Linked to identity via `exhibits` relation edge.
    47→
    48→### F4: Exhibits Relation (Graph Layer)
    49→New TYPE RELATION table `exhibits` (IN identity OUT behavior). Created by Observer Agent when writing behavior records. One identity exhibits many behaviors over time.
    50→
    51→### F5: Authorizer-Objective Integration (Agent Layer)
    52→Authorizer Agent extended to evaluate intent-objective alignment during authorization. Semantic similarity between intent.goal embedding and active objective embeddings. Warning mode (observation) when no match; blocking mode (deny) as optional policy.
    53→
    54→### F6: Policy-Behavior Integration (Policy Layer)
    55→Policy rules extended to reference behavior metrics. Policy condition type: behavior metric threshold (e.g., "Security_First < 0.8 -> deny production_deploy"). Authorizer queries latest behavior scores during policy evaluation.
    56→
    57→### F7: Behavior Dashboard (UI Layer)
    58→Web UI showing per-agent behavior scores, trends (UP/DOWN/FLAT), threshold violations. Detail view with score history, source telemetry, sparkline charts. Accessible from workspace navigation.
    59→
    60→### F8: Objective Progress View (UI Layer)
    61→Web UI showing objective title, target date, progress bar, key results, supporting intents count, unaligned intents count. Accessible from workspace navigation and governance feed.
    62→
    63→### F9: Strategic Alignment Report (UI Layer)
    64→Monthly report view showing per-objective compute allocation percentages, unaligned intent categories, recommendations. Exportable for board presentations.
    65→
    66→### F10: Coherence Auditor (Agent Layer)
    67→Extension of Observer Agent (or dedicated X-Ray Agent) to run periodic graph queries detecting disconnected patterns: objectives with no supporting intents (14-day threshold), decisions with no implementing tasks, tasks with no outcome observations. Creates observations with appropriate severity.
    68→
    69→### F11: Observer Behavior Extension + Learning Integration (Agent Layer)
    70→Observer Agent extended to consume behavior records as input signals for its existing learning proposal pipeline (PR #145). When the Observer detects `behavioral_drift` patterns from behavior telemetry, it proposes targeted learnings for the underperforming agent via the existing learning API (`POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings`). Learnings are proposed with status `pending_approval`, pass three-layer collision detection and dual-gate safety, and are injected into agent system prompts via JIT prompt injection after human approval.
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→## Non-Functional Requirements
    75→
    76→### Performance
    77→- Objective alignment evaluation completes within 200ms (must not add perceptible latency to intent authorization)
    78→- Behavior dashboard loads within 2 seconds for workspaces with up to 50 agents and 10,000 behavior records
    79→- Coherence auditor completes full scan within 30 seconds for workspaces with up to 5,000 nodes
    80→
    81→### Security
    82→- Objective and behavior data scoped to workspace (existing workspace isolation model applies)
    83→- Human override of behavior policy veto logged with identity for audit trail
    84→- Policy creation restricted to human identities (agents cannot create or modify behavior policies)
    85→
    86→### Reliability
    87→- Behavior scoring failure (Observer Agent down) must not block agent session execution
    88→- Objective matching failure must not block intent authorization in warning mode
    89→- Coherence auditor failures logged as observations, not silently swallowed
    90→
    91→### Data Integrity
    92→- Behavior records are append-only (scores never retroactively modified)
    93→- Objective status transitions are auditable (status change logged with timestamp and actor)
    94→- Supports edges are immutable once created (intent-objective link is permanent record)
    95→
    96→---
    97→
    98→## Business Rules
    99→
   100→### BR-1: Objective Lifecycle
   101→- Objectives are created by humans only (extraction from chat or direct creation)
   102→- Agents cannot create, modify, or archive objectives autonomously
   103→- Objective with target_date in the past transitions to "expired" (coherence auditor)
   104→- Completed objectives require human confirmation
   105→
   106→### BR-2: Alignment Evaluation
   107→- Alignment uses semantic similarity (cosine distance) between intent.goal embedding and objective embeddings
   108→- Threshold for automatic linking: similarity >= 0.7
   109→- Threshold for warning: similarity < 0.5 for all active objectives
   110→- Ambiguous matches (multiple objectives >= 0.5) linked to highest score, surfaced for human confirmation
   111→
   112→### BR-3: Behavior Scoring
   113→- One behavior record per agent session per metric type (not retroactively modifiable)
   114→- Score range: 0.0 to 1.0 (normalized by Observer Agent)
   115→- Trends computed from last 5 records minimum (insufficient data shows "--")
   116→- New agents have no behavior data until first session completes
   117→
   118→### BR-4: Policy Enforcement
   119→- Behavior-based policy rules evaluate against latest behavior score (not average)
   120→- Policy status "testing" means observe-only (log what would be vetoed, do not block)
   121→- Policy status "active" means enforce (block intent if condition met)
   122→- Human override of veto always available; override logged with identity
   123→
   124→### BR-5: Observer Behavior Learning
   125→- Learning proposed only for behavior trends (3+ consecutive below-threshold sessions), not single scores
   126→- Observer proposes via existing learning API with source "agent", suggested_by "observer", status "pending_approval"
   127→- Learning type is "instruction" for behavioral guidance, "constraint" for hard safety rules
   128→- Three-layer collision detection prevents duplicate/contradictory learnings (existing infrastructure)
   129→- Dual-gate safety: rate limit (5 per agent per 7 days) + dismissed similarity check (0.85) (existing infrastructure)
   130→- Human approval required before learning becomes active (pending_approval -> active)
   131→- Learning effectiveness measured by score improvement after injection (behavior records as evidence)
   132→- Ineffective learnings (no improvement after review period) surfaced for human review via observations
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## Dependencies
   137→
   138→### Existing Infrastructure (Available)
   139→- Intent system with authorization pipeline (intent table, Authorizer Agent)
   140→- Policy system with rules evaluation (policy table)
   141→- Identity system with agent types (identity table, agent table)
   142→- Observation system with lifecycle (observation table, observes edge)
   143→- Embedding generation pipeline
   144→- SSE event streaming for feed cards
   145→- Graph visualization (Reagraph)
   146→
   147→### Existing Infrastructure (Available -- Learning System, PR #145)
   148→- `learning` table: text, learning_type (constraint|instruction|precedent), status (active|pending_approval|dismissed|superseded|deactivated), source (human|agent), priority, target_agents, suggested_by, pattern_confidence, embedding
   149→- `learning_evidence` relation: links learning to observation/message/trace/agent_session
   150→- `supersedes` relation: links new learning to old learning
   151→- Learning CRUD API: POST/GET/PUT `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/learnings`, actions endpoint for approve/dismiss/deactivate/supersede
   152→- Three-layer collision detection: learning vs learning (0.90), vs policy (0.40, hard block on contradiction), vs decision (0.55)
   153→- Observer learning proposal pipeline: clusters observations, root cause analysis (policy_failure|context_failure|behavioral_drift), proposes learnings with dual-gate safety (rate limit 5/agent/7 days + dismissed similarity 0.85)
   154→- JIT prompt injection: learnings loaded into agent prompts at runtime with 500-token budget (constraints always, instructions by priority, precedents by similarity)
   155→- Learning Library UI: browse, filter, approve, edit, deactivate learnings
   156→
   157→### New Infrastructure Required
   158→- `objective` table + schema migration
   159→- `behavior` table + schema migration
   160→- `supports` relation table + schema migration
   161→- `exhibits` relation table + schema migration
   162→- Observer Agent extension for behavior telemetry collection
   163→- Authorizer Agent extension for objective alignment evaluation
   164→- Policy evaluation extension for behavior metric conditions
   165→
   166→### External Dependencies
   167→- None (all telemetry sources are internal to Brain -- agent session data, trace records)
   168→- Future: GitHub API integration for richer source telemetry (PR data, CI results) -- out of scope for initial release
   169→
   170→---
   171→
   172→## Glossary
   173→
   174→| Term | Definition |
   175→|------|-----------|
   176→| **Objective** | A strategic goal node in the graph. Every agent action should trace to an objective. |
   177→| **Behavior** | A record of agent process quality (craftsmanship) for a specific metric type and session. |
   178→| **Alignment Score** | Semantic similarity (0-1) between an intent's goal and an objective's description. |
   179→| **Organizational Waste** | Agent compute spent on work that does not support any active objective. |
   180→| **Craftsmanship** | Quality of agent process (how work is done), distinct from output quality (what is produced). |
   181→| **Behavior Threshold** | Minimum acceptable behavior score defined in a policy rule. |
   182→| **Coherence Score** | Ratio of properly-connected nodes to total nodes in a workspace graph. |
   183→| **Exhibits Edge** | Graph relation linking an identity to a behavior record (identity ->exhibits-> behavior). |
   184→| **Supports Edge** | Graph relation linking an intent to an objective (intent ->supports-> objective). |
   185→

1→# User Stories: Objective & Behavior Nodes
     2→
     3→---
     4→
     5→## US-OB-01: Create Strategic Objectives
     6→
     7→### Problem
     8→Elena Vasquez is an engineering lead managing 4 autonomous coding agents. She finds it impossible to know whether agent work serves business goals because there is no concept of "objectives" in the knowledge graph. She currently trusts agents to stay on-task via project context, but last week Coder-Alpha spent 4 hours refactoring a utility module while the Q2 launch feature was understaffed.
     9→
    10→### Who
    11→- Engineering Lead | Managing autonomous agents | Wants strategic alignment and compute waste prevention
    12→
    13→### Solution
    14→Enable creating objective nodes in the knowledge graph via chat conversation or direct creation, with title, description, target date, success criteria, priority, and workspace scoping.
    15→
    16→### Domain Examples
    17→
    18→#### 1: Happy Path -- Elena creates Q2 launch objective via chat
    19→Elena sends: "Our Q2 objective is to launch the MCP marketplace with 10 listed integrations by June 30." Brain extracts an objective node with title "Launch MCP Marketplace", target_date 2026-06-30, success_criteria ["10 listed integrations"], priority 90, status "active". Elena sees confirmation with extracted fields.
    20→
    21→#### 2: Edge Case -- Objective without target date
    22→Elena sends: "We need to improve infrastructure reliability this quarter." Brain creates the objective but prompts: "What is the target date for this objective?" The objective is created with status "active" and no target_date until Elena provides one.
    23→
    24→#### 3: Error/Boundary -- Duplicate objective detection
    25→Elena sends: "Our goal is to launch the MCP marketplace." Brain detects semantic similarity 0.97 with existing active objective "Launch MCP Marketplace." Instead of creating a duplicate, Brain responds: "An active objective 'Launch MCP Marketplace' already exists. Would you like to update it?"
    26→
    27→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
    28→
    29→#### Scenario: Create objective from chat message
    30→Given Elena Vasquez is in a conversation in workspace "BrainOS"
    31→When Elena sends "Our Q2 objective is to launch the MCP marketplace with 10 listed integrations by June 30"
    32→Then an objective node is created with title "Launch MCP Marketplace"
    33→And the objective has target_date 2026-06-30
    34→And the objective has success_criteria containing "10 listed integrations"
    35→And the objective has status "active" and priority 90
    36→And the objective has an embedding generated for semantic matching
    37→
    38→#### Scenario: Prompt for missing target date
    39→Given Elena sends "We need to improve infrastructure reliability this quarter"
    40→When the extraction pipeline creates the objective
    41→Then the objective is created without target_date
    42→And the system prompts Elena for a target date
    43→
    44→#### Scenario: Detect and prevent duplicate objectives
    45→Given objective "Launch MCP Marketplace" exists with status "active"
    46→When Elena sends "Our goal is to launch the MCP marketplace"
    47→Then no duplicate objective is created
    48→And Elena sees: "An active objective 'Launch MCP Marketplace' already exists"
    49→
    50→#### Scenario: Objective created with workspace scope and embedding
    51→Given Elena is in workspace "BrainOS"
    52→When an objective is created
    53→Then the objective has workspace reference to "BrainOS"
    54→And the objective has an embedding for semantic matching
    55→And the objective is only visible within workspace "BrainOS"
    56→
    57→### Acceptance Criteria
    58→- [ ] Objective node created from chat with extracted title, target_date, success_criteria, priority
    59→- [ ] Missing target_date prompts user (objective still created)
    60→- [ ] Duplicate detection prevents creating semantically identical objectives (similarity > 0.95)
    61→- [ ] Objective scoped to workspace with embedding generated
    62→
    63→### Technical Notes
    64→- New SCHEMAFULL table `objective` in SurrealDB schema
    65→- Extraction pipeline extended to recognize objective-type entities
    66→- Embedding generation reuses existing pipeline (same dimension/model)
    67→- Status enum: active, expired, completed, archived
    68→
    69→### Dependencies
    70→- Embedding generation pipeline (existing)
    71→- Extraction pipeline (existing, needs extension)
    72→- Schema migration (new table)
    73→
    74→### Job Story Trace
    75→- J1: Strategic Alignment Governance
    76→
    77→### Size Estimate
    78→- Effort: 2-3 days
    79→- Scenarios: 4
    80→- Demonstrable: Create objective via chat, see it in graph
    81→
    82→---
    83→
    84→## US-OB-02: Automatic Intent-Objective Alignment
    85→
    86→### Problem
    87→Elena Vasquez cannot tell whether agent intents serve active business objectives. Even with objectives defined, there is no mechanism to connect intents to objectives. She discovers misalignment only through manual review of agent activity, often days after compute is spent.
    88→
    89→### Who
    90→- Engineering Lead | Reviewing agent strategic alignment | Wants automatic alignment tracking without manual overhead
    91→
    92→### Solution
    93→Authorizer Agent evaluates intent-objective alignment during authorization by computing semantic similarity between the intent goal and active objective embeddings. Creates `supports` edge when aligned; creates warning observation when unaligned.
    94→
    95→### Domain Examples
    96→
    97→#### 1: Happy Path -- Intent automatically linked to matching objective
    98→Coder-Alpha submits intent "Implement MCP tool discovery endpoint." Authorizer computes similarity with active objectives, finds "Launch MCP Marketplace" at 0.87. Creates `supports` edge: intent ->supports-> objective. Intent proceeds to authorization.
    99→
   100→#### 2: Edge Case -- No matching objective found
   101→Coder-Beta submits intent "Refactor logging subsystem to use structured logs." Best similarity score is 0.31 (with "Improve Infrastructure Reliability"). Authorizer creates observation (severity: warning): "Intent has no supporting objective. Potential organizational waste." Intent proceeds (warning mode). Feed card appears with "Link to Objective", "Dismiss", "Create Objective" actions.
   102→
   103→#### 3: Error/Boundary -- Ambiguous match (multiple objectives)
   104→Coder-Alpha submits intent "Add health checks to MCP service endpoints." Two objectives match: "Launch MCP Marketplace" (0.72) and "Improve Infrastructure Reliability" (0.68). System links to highest score, surfaces both in feed card for Elena to confirm or reassign.
   105→
   106→### UAT Scenarios (BDD)
   107→
   108→#### Scenario: Intent linked to matching objective
   109→Given objective "Launch MCP Marketplace" exists with status "active" and embedding
   110→When Coder-Alpha submits intent with goal "Implement MCP tool discovery endpoint"
   111→Then the Authorizer computes semantic similarity above 0.7
   112→And a supports edge is created: intent ->supports-> objective "Launch MCP Marketplace"
   113→And the intent proceeds to authorization evaluation
   114→
   115→#### Scenario: Unaligned intent triggers warning
   116→Given objective "Launch MCP Marketplace" is the only active objective
   117→When Coder-Beta submits intent with goal "Refactor logging subsystem to use structured logs"
   118→Then the Authorizer finds no objective match above 0.5
   119→And an observation is created with severity "warning"
   120→And the intent is NOT blocked (warning mode)
   121→And a feed card appears with link/dismiss/create actions
   122→
   123→#### Scenario: Elena manually links unaligned intent from feed card
   124→Given an alignment warning exists for intent "Refactor logging subsystem"
   125→When Elena clicks "Link to Objective" and selects "Improve Infrastructure Reliability"
   126→Then a supports edge is created between intent and selected objective
   127→And the warning observation is resolved
   128→
   129→#### Scenario: No objectives exist in workspace
   130→Given no objectives exist in workspace "BrainOS"
   131→When Coder-Alpha submits an intent
   132→Then a feed card appears: "No objectives defined. Agent work is untracked."
   133→And the intent proceeds without alignment check
   134→
   135→#### Scenario: Ambiguous match surfaces both options
   136→Given two objectives match with similarity above 0.5
   137→When the intent is evaluated
   138→Then it links to the highest-scoring objective
   139→And a feed card shows both options for human confirmation
   140→
   141→### Acceptance Criteria
   142→- [ ] Authorizer computes intent-objective alignment via semantic similarity
   143→- [ ] Supports edge created for similarity >= 0.7
   144→- [ ] Warning observation for similarity < 0.5 across all objectives
   145→- [ ] Manual linking via feed card actions resolves warning
   146→- [ ] No objectives: intent proceeds with informational feed card
   147→- [ ] Alignment evaluation completes within 200ms
   148→
   149→### Technical Notes
   150→- Authorizer needs access to active objective embeddings (workspace-scoped KNN query)
   151→- Supports relation table: TYPE RELATION IN intent OUT objective
   152→- Warning observation uses existing observation table with category "engineering"
   153→- Feed card actions require SSE event and API endpoint for manual linking
   154→
   155→### Dependencies
   156→- US-OB-01 (Objective nodes must exist)
   157→- Intent authorization pipeline (existing)
   158→- Embedding pipeline (existing)
   159→
   160→### Job Story Trace
   161→- J1: Strategic Alignment Governance
   162→- J4: Objective-Driven Cost Governance
   163→
   164→### Size Estimate
   165→- Effort: 2-3 days
   166→- Scenarios: 5
   167→- Demonstrable: Submit intent, see supports edge or warning feed card
